item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly a parallel universe, seeing how onto-kinetic he is.

item.title: Great SCP!
item.content:encoded:
The 100+ year setting, detailed present tense plot and character development all add up to a fascinating document. +1 (However, the object class is a bit confusing).

This part is my favorite:

"Second Mexican Revolution in 2147. At the height of the Revolution, SCP-4130 had made its base in the city of Guanajuato after a failed attempt at capturing Mexico City, and formed a quasi-government with considerable recognized authority in the south of Mexico, with himself in the leader position as "True Governor Of All States In The Republic Of Mexico". During its campaigns in the Second Revolution, SCP-4130 would often engage combatants with its reality-bending capabilities, necessitating extensive Foundation cover-up and distribution of amnestics."

item.title: AMLOVE
item.content:encoded:
This line is actually scary:

The battle saw SCP-4000's victory followed by the establishment of his "True Government" as Mexico's leading political force.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this one. It's a little rough in places, and I'm not a big fan of the repeated crossing-out of previous Object Classes, but otherwise this tells a cool story. Your hook of the last sentence of the containment procedures and first sentence of the description is great and this whole thing is just enjoyable.

I'm a little dubious about the willingness of the Foundation to send advanced technology back in time, especially to an organisation that isn't actually the Foundation yet, but I can write that off easily enough since it's set far in the future itself (and I'm sure similar stuff has been done before), but that's my only major issue with this. Beyond that, I would have liked to see a little more detail in what actually did happen with this guy once he was in the past (what history did they have to edit).

Otherwise, this is a fun little story. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I share Conwell’s opinion here. This really didn’t build enough momentum for me; I feel like I would’ve enjoyed it more if it did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. I didn't get a whole lot out of this, to be honest.

While the idea of Santa Anna as a time-traveling reality bender is somewhat interesting, as is the Foundation reaching out to a past predecessor in an attempt to pass the buck, the bulk of the narrative itself boils down to so much dry "Reality Bender escapes Foundation: Episode N" without a whole lot of characterization of Santa Anna or the Foundation to make me care for the outcome, which is fairly essential for a character-focused piece as this.

No vote. Several nuggets here are good, but they don't fully solidify for me as a reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was entertaining and went through some nice classic themes of time travel and causality. I would have preferred for a 4000 article to be broader in scope, as this reads more like a standard mainlister, but I was comfortable upvoting in spite of that reservation.

My minor nitpick is that having multiple separate pages (particularly with separate voting modules) is something I was happy to see people lose interest in after the earlier series, if only for pedantic reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I quite like this one, especially with the implication of a bootstrap paradox. (both on the part of Santa Anna, but also in part the Foundation and reality anchoring technology).
To be honest, I half expected the link at the end of the tale to go to a different version of the article.

item.title: SCP PATCH NOTES 1.1
item.content:encoded: • By popular demand, "Less Bloodthirsty Foundation" mode has been enabled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yaknow, despite the destroy, destroy, destroy criticism, this tells a really good story, and that's all I needed. Like, I could totally buy that, for whatever reason, they just had to kill this guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon
– hide block

The devices future Foundation sent to the past retroactively changes how the Foundation deals with anomalies.

In the timeline without 19th century Santa Anna the Foundation have no effective method to counter reality benders at least until the 22th century with the invention of device like the reality anchor. Because of that their method in dealing with reality bending anomalies is more brutish simply out of necessity.

However, with the introduction of future anti reality bending devices to the past, greatly improving their containment capabilities and allows the to take a more "softer" approach at anomalies. This leads to the current Foundation, with their aversion to neutralizing anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for informing me! I'll do an editing pass tomorrow probably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, this contest you can make edits. (I'm not an official contest minion, but I'm 99% sure you can, and others have made edits as well)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yknow, that's fair and I didn't really stop to think about this while writing. I don't think I can edit it until the contest is over but I'll give it a pass once it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little concerned about the Foundation's 'shoot first' approach here.

As a pretty hard rule, the Foundation does not terminate entities. Secure, Contain, Protect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Time paradoxes are always fun, and this is a solid example of the bootstrap paradox. Where did Santa Anna come from? More importantly, when did he come from?
